message.content:
Here is a consolidated “canon” of primary, published sources and standards you would rely on to test **HTTP security headers**, **TLS/HTTPS configuration**, and **web infrastructure security** for web apps. I’ll group them by theme and note for each:

- **Name**
- **Organization / publisher**
- **What it defines (test categories / checks)**
- **Canonical URL** (as specified in the source itself).

---

## 1. HTTP Security Headers – Core Guidance and Test References

### 1.1 OWASP Secure Headers Project

- **Name:** OWASP Secure Headers Project  
- **Organization:** OWASP (Open Worldwide Application Security Project)  
- **What it defines:**  
  - Catalog of **security-relevant HTTP response headers** and recommended values, including HSTS (`Strict-Transport-Security`), CSP (`Content-Security-Policy`), `X-Content-Type-Options`, `X-Frame-Options`, `Referrer-Policy`, `Feature-Policy` / `Permissions-Policy`, `X-XSS-Protection` (legacy), etc.[1]  
  - Provides **baseline configurations** for common web frameworks and servers.  
  - Serves as a reference for **what headers should be present, with what values**, for testing and benchmarking hardening of web apps.  
- **Canonical URL:** OWASP Secure Headers Project is linked from the OWASP HTTP Headers Cheat Sheet as the reference project.[1]

### 1.2 OWASP HTTP Headers Cheat Sheet

- **Name:** HTTP Headers Cheat Sheet  
- **Organization:** OWASP Cheat Sheet Series  
- **What it defines:**  
  - Practical **testing and configuration guidance** for nearly all modern security headers.[1]  
  - For each header: purpose, **recommended configuration**, security considerations, and external standards references (e.g., CSP spec, HSTS RFCs).[1]  
  - Explicit guidance on **removing deprecated headers** (e.g., HPKP), defense-in-depth behavior, and common misconfigurations.[1]  
  - Frequently used as a de facto **checklist** for secure header testing.  
- **Canonical URL:**  
  - `https://cheatsheetseries.owasp.org/cheatsheets/HTTP_Headers_Cheat_Sheet.html`[1]

---

## 2. Mozilla Observatory & Mozilla Web Security Guidelines

### 2.1 Mozilla HTTP Observatory

- **Name:** HTTP Observatory  
- **Organization:** Mozilla / MDN Web Docs  
- **What it defines:**  
  - An automated **HTTP header and TLS configuration scanner**.[4][6]  
  - Tests presence/quality of:
    - **HTTP security headers** (CSP, HSTS, X-Content-Type-Options, X-Frame-Options, Referrer-Policy, etc.).[4]  
    - **TLS configuration**: protocol versions, ciphers, key exchange, certificate properties (via integrated external scanners like SSL Labs).[6]  
    - Other **web security best practices** (e.g., redirection patterns, preload, mixed content).[4]  
  - Provides a **grading model** (A+…F) with **actionable recommendations** to fix findings.[4]  
- **Canonical URL:**  
  - `https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/observatory`[4]

### 2.2 Mozilla Web Security Guidelines (security baseline)

- **Name:** Mozilla Web Security Guidelines / Security Baseline  
- **Organization:** Mozilla (referenced by Observatory and MDN)  
- **What it defines:**  
  - General **baseline for secure configuration** of web applications and servers used by Mozilla.  
  - Categories include:
    - **Transport security** (HTTPS-only, HSTS, TLS parameters)  
    - **Content security** (CSP, XFO, X-Content-Type-Options, etc.)  
    - **Cookie settings** (Secure, HttpOnly, SameSite)  
  - These guidelines are the **policy foundation for Observatory’s tests and scoring**.[4][6]  
- **Canonical URL:**  
  - Linked from the Mozilla Observatory landing page as “documentation / guidelines” for the scan results.[4]

---

## 3. CSP (Content Security Policy) – MDN & W3C Specs

### 3.1 MDN Web Docs – Content Security Policy Reference

- **Name:** Content Security Policy (CSP) – MDN Reference  
- **Organization:** Mozilla / MDN Web Docs  
- **What it defines:**  
  - Comprehensive **developer-focused reference** for CSP headers and directives: `Content-Security-Policy`, `Content-Security-Policy-Report-Only`, all major directives (`default-src`, `script-src`, `style-src`, `frame-ancestors`, `connect-src`, etc.).[3]  
  - Documents **browser support**, typical **usage patterns**, and how to interpret/report CSP violations.  
  - Used as a **practical testing reference** to determine whether a given CSP configuration is sound and aligned with browser behavior.  
- **Canonical URL:**  
  - Linked as “Content Security Policy Reference” from OWASP HTTP Headers Cheat Sheet.[1]

### 3.2 W3C Content Security Policy Specifications

- **Name:** Content Security Policy Level 3 (and earlier levels)  
- **Organization:** W3C (World Wide Web Consortium) – Web Application Security Working Group  
- **What it defines:**  
  - The **formal CSP standard**:
    - Defines the semantics of CSP directives, source lists, reporting, violation handling, and enforcement model.  
    - Specifies the behavior of `Content-Security-Policy` and `Content-Security-Policy-Report-Only` headers.  
  - Basis for **conformance tests** and normative interpretation of whether an implementation / configuration is standard-compliant.  
- **Canonical URL:**  
  - The current W3C CSP specification is referenced from MDN and OWASP as the standard CSP spec.[1][3]

---

## 4. TLS / HTTPS Configuration Standards & Testing Guides

### 4.1 Mozilla SSL Configuration Generator & TLS Guidelines

- **Name:** Mozilla SSL Configuration Generator  
- **Organization:** Mozilla  
- **What it defines:**  
  - Opinionated **recommended TLS configurations** for common servers (Apache, Nginx, HAProxy, etc.), categorized by:
    - **Modern**, **Intermediate**, and **Old** compatibility profiles.  
  - Specifies:
    - **TLS protocol versions** to enable/disable  
    - Approved **cipher suites**, key exchange mechanisms, and curves  
    - Recommended **HSTS** and **OCSP stapling** settings  
  - Used as a **canonical baseline** for TLS test expectations (what is “good” vs “legacy/weak”).  
- **Canonical URL:**  
  - Linked from the Mozilla Observatory and MDN as “Mozilla SSL Configuration Generator.”[4]

- **Name:** Mozilla Server Side TLS / TLS Guidelines  
- **Organization:** Mozilla  
- **What it defines:**  
  - Detailed **TLS/HTTPS hardening guidelines**:
    - Deprecated protocols (SSLv3, TLS 1.0/1.1)  
    - Requirements for **forward secrecy**, key sizes, certificate algorithms  
    - Minimums for different environments (web, mail, etc.)  
  - Serves as a widely used **authoritative standard** for TLS test criteria (e.g., when building security scanners or policies).  
- **Canonical URL:**  
  - Referenced by the SSL Configuration Generator and the Mozilla Observatory as the underlying policy document.[4]

### 4.2 Qualys SSL Labs – SSL Server Rating Guide

- **Name:** SSL Server Rating Guide  
- **Organization:** Qualys SSL Labs  
- **What it defines:**  
  - The **scoring methodology** used by SSL Labs’ SSL Server Test.  
  - Categories for evaluation:  
    - **Protocol support** (enabled TLS/SSL versions)  
    - **Key exchange** (key sizes, parameters)  
    - **Cipher strength** (cipher suites, forward secrecy)  
    - **Certificate** quality and trust status  
    - Penalties for known issues (e.g., insecure renegotiation, RC4, 3DES, weak DH params).  
  - Specifies how each category contributes to the **overall A+–F grade**, enabling reproducible TLS configuration testing.  
- **Canonical URL:**  
  - Linked from the SSL Labs main site as “SSL Server Rating Guide.”

---

## 5. Cookie Security Attribute Standards

### 5.1 RFC 6265 and RFC 6265bis (HTTP State Management Mechanism)

- **Name:** HTTP State Management Mechanism (RFC 6265 / 6265bis)  
- **Organization:** IETF (Internet Engineering Task Force) – HTTP Working Group  
- **What it defines:**  
  - The **normative standard** for HTTP cookies.  
  - Defines:
    - `Set-Cookie` and `Cookie` syntax and semantics  
    - **Security attributes**:  
      - `Secure` (only over HTTPS)  
      - `HttpOnly` (not accessible via client-side script)  
      - `SameSite` (`Strict`, `Lax`, `None`), including requirements that `SameSite=None` must also have `Secure`  
    - Path, domain scoping, expiration, and storage rules  
  - Basis for **testing cookie configurations**:
    - Checking for presence/absence of `Secure`, `HttpOnly`, `SameSite` on session cookies  
    - Ensuring compliance with new `SameSite` defaults in modern browsers.  
- **Canonical URL:**  
  - The draft/updated spec for **RFC 6265bis** is published as the IETF HTTP State Management Mechanism revision, referenced from MDN cookie docs and browser implementer notes.

### 5.2 MDN – Set-Cookie and Cookie Reference

- **Name:** `Set-Cookie` – HTTP Header Reference  
- **Organization:** Mozilla / MDN Web Docs  
- **What it defines:**  
  - Practical **developer-grade documentation** of the cookie standard:
    - Usage of `Secure`, `HttpOnly`, `SameSite`  
    - Security implications of each attribute  
  - Used as a **testing reference** for verifying secure cookie settings and browser behavior.  
- **Canonical URL:**  
  - Linked as “Set-Cookie” under MDN HTTP headers documentation.[1]

---

## 6. CORS (Cross-Origin Resource Sharing) Specifications

### 6.1 Fetch Standard / CORS Sections

- **Name:** Fetch Standard – CORS  
- **Organization:** WHATWG (Web Hypertext Application Technology Working Group)  
- **What it defines:**  
  - The **authoritative CORS algorithm** used by browsers:
    - Preflight requests (OPTIONS)  
    - `Access-Control-Allow-Origin`, `Access-Control-Allow-Credentials`, `Access-Control-Allow-Headers`, `Access-Control-Allow-Methods`, etc.  
    - Simple vs non-simple requests, credentialed requests, and error handling.  
  - Forms the basis for **testing CORS behavior**:
    - Verifying that only intended origins are allowed  
    - Ensuring credentials are not allowed with `*` origins, etc.  
- **Canonical URL:**  
  - The Fetch Standard document, which has the normative CORS processing model.

### 6.2 W3C “Cross-Origin Resource Sharing” (historic spec)

- **Name:** Cross-Origin Resource Sharing  
- **Organization:** W3C  
- **What it defines:**  
  - Earlier **CORS specification** that laid out:
    - CORS request/response headers  
    - Preflight, actual request, and error handling flows  
  - Still used as a secondary reference; modern browsers follow the Fetch Standard, but the W3C spec remains a **canonical document** often cited in security literature and testing references.  
- **Canonical URL:**  
  - W3C’s “Cross-Origin Resource Sharing” specification, referenced from MDN CORS documentation.

### 6.3 MDN – CORS Reference

- **Name:** HTTP access control (CORS) / CORS – MDN Docs  
- **Organization:** Mozilla / MDN Web Docs  
- **What it defines:**  
  - High-level **description of CORS headers** and configuration:
    - `Access-Control-Allow-Origin`  
    - `Access-Control-Allow-Credentials`  
    - `Access-Control-Allow-Headers` / `Allow-Methods`  
    - `Access-Control-Expose-Headers`  
  - Practical guidance on **correct vs insecure CORS setups** for testers and developers.  
- **Canonical URL:**  
  - MDN’s CORS page, linked as “CORS” and “HTTP access control (CORS)” in MDN HTTP documentation.

---

## 7. OWASP Testing Guide – Config / Transport / Infrastructure Sections

### 7.1 OWASP Web Security Testing Guide (WSTG) v4.x

- **Name:** OWASP Web Security Testing Guide (WSTG)  
- **Organization:** OWASP  
- **What it defines:**  
  - A comprehensive manual for **web application security testing**, including dedicated sections on config and transport:  
    - **WSTG-CONFIG** – Configuration and Deployment Management Testing  
      - Testing for **HTTP security headers** (e.g., CSP, XFO, X-Content-Type-Options, HSTS) as part of configuration assessment.  
      - Server/banner leakage, default configurations, directory listing, file permissions.  
    - **WSTG-INFO** – Information Gathering  
      - Identifying **server software**, TLS endpoints, supported protocols & ciphers.  
    - **WSTG-CRYPST** – Cryptography / Transport layer  
      - Testing **HTTPS/TLS**:
        - Protocol downgrade vulnerabilities  
        - Weak ciphers and key sizes  
        - Certificate validation and trust.  
    - **WSTG-CLNT** – Client-Side Testing  
      - Includes tests for **CSP**, **CORS**, and other browser security controls via headers.  
  - Provides **test objectives, methodology, and example test cases** that map directly to the security headers and TLS configuration concerns you’re interested in.  
- **Canonical URL:**  
  - OWASP Testing Guide main page, which links to each chapter/section in HTML and PDF form.

---

## 8. Additional Canonical Header/Transport Standards (IETF)

For completeness, here are key RFCs that are canonical for specific security headers and HTTPS behavior; they are often *implicitly* referenced by the above guides and used as ground truth for “correctness” in tests:

- **HSTS (HTTP Strict Transport Security)**  
  - **Name:** HTTP Strict Transport Security (HSTS)  
  - **Organization:** IETF  
  - **Defines:** `Strict-Transport-Security` header, semantics of `max-age`, `includeSubDomains`, preload considerations, and browser behavior enforcing HTTPS-only access.  
  - **Canonical URL:** RFC defining HSTS, referenced by MDN and OWASP HTTP Headers Cheat Sheet.[1]

- **Referrer Policy**  
  - **Name:** Referrer Policy  
  - **Organization:** W3C  
  - **Defines:** `Referrer-Policy` header and `<meta>` equivalents; values like `no-referrer`, `same-origin`, `strict-origin-when-cross-origin` and their privacy/security impact.  
  - **Canonical URL:** W3C Referrer Policy spec, referenced from MDN and OWASP.[1][2]

- **Permissions Policy (formerly Feature Policy)**  
  - **Name:** Permissions Policy  
  - **Organization:** W3C  
  - **Defines:** `Permissions-Policy` header (and legacy `Feature-Policy`) for controlling use of powerful browser features (e.g., camera, geolocation, fullscreen).  
  - **Canonical URL:** W3C Permissions Policy spec, referenced from MDN header docs.[1]

- **HTTP/1.1 and HTTP Semantics**  
  - **Name:** HTTP Semantics / HTTP/1.1 (RFC 7230–7235 and successors)  
  - **Organization:** IETF HTTP Working Group  
  - **Defines:** General **HTTP header semantics**, status codes, and message formats; baseline for validating that security headers are syntactically correct and compliant.  
  - **Canonical URL:** IETF HTTP Semantics RFC, referenced by MDN HTTP documentation.

---

## 9. Other Well-Recognized Web Security Header / Config References

These are not standards bodies per se, but are widely used as **testing/guideline anchors** in practice and often cited:

- **securityheaders.com**  
  - **Organization:** Scott Helme  
  - **Defines:** An automated scanner and scoring model for **HTTP security headers** (presence and configuration of CSP, HSTS, XFO, X-CTO, RP, etc.), similar in spirit to Mozilla Observatory.[2]  
  - **Canonical URL:** `https://securityheaders.com`, referenced from educational material on HTTP security headers.[2]

- **Invicti / PortSwigger / other vendor “HTTP Security Headers” references** – educational but not canonical standards, useful for test explanations, not normative.[5][7]

---

If you want, I can next:

- Map each of these sources into **a checklist** (e.g., “for HTTP response headers, use OWASP Secure Headers + OWASP HTTP Headers Cheat Sheet + WSTG-CONFIG, plus MDN + W3C specs as normative references”) or  
- Build a **crosswalk table**: header / TLS aspect → canonical spec(s) → practical testing guide(s) → automated tool(s) (Observatory, SSL Labs, securityheaders.com).
